notes: Security measures: On 14 January 2021, North Korea held a military parade in Kim Il Sung Square, Pyongyang, to celebrate the end of the Workers' Party's Eighth Congress. Some previously unseen weapons, including submarine-launched ballistic missile (SLBM) and short-range ballistic missiles (SRBMs) were displayed during the parade. This is seen by many as a show of force to demonstrate their nuclear weapon capabilities, especially as it came just days before the inauguration of U.S. President-elect Joe Biden. It also worries the U.S and neighboring countries as the SLBMs are harder to detect in advance. However, the actual capabilities of these weapons have not yet been revealed or publicly tested.

On 10 October 2020, North Korea held a military parade in Pyongyang to celebrate the 75th anniversary of the founding of the Workers' Party of Korea. The parade included a new ICBM, said by analysts to likely be the largest road-mobile ICBM to have ever been built and possibly capable of holding multiple warheads with multiple re-entry vehicle capability; North Korea has not yet publicly tested this ICBM. Also on display was North Korea's previous largest missile capable of carrying a nuclear warhead, the Hwasong-15, as well as a new SLBM dubbed the Pukguksong-4. Other signs of modernization in North Korea's conventional forces were evident. The country did not display any nuclear-capable missiles during its last military parade in September 2019 during peace negotiations with South Korea and the United States.

On 25 August 2020, North Korean authorities ordered the suspension of all trade across the Sino-North Korean border in an effort to ensure that a military parade and mass rally celebrating the 75th anniversary of the founding of the communist party to be held on October 10 be unaffected by coronavirus fears. Although the border had already been officially shut down in January 2020, state-sponsored smuggling activities were still ongoing. In addition, on 27 August, local military and police units put into effect an order from the Ministry of State Security to shoot on sight anyone within one kilometre of the Sino-Korean border. This emergency declaration will reportedly be in effect until the pandemic ends.

On 25 August 2020, according to anonymous sources in North Korea, the Reconnaissance General Bureau (RGB) deployed around 500 troops to Pyongyang in an effort to protect secret RGB military facilities from damage during Typhoon Bavi. Other critical facilities in Pyongyang were protected by the Pyongyang Defense Command, Artillery Guidance Bureau, and other military units. Many analysts see North Korea's weapons developments as a tactic to secure sanctions relief and political concessions from the United States.

This marks the first ballistic missile provocation since the start of the Donald Trump administration and is seen as a response to the US and South Korea's joint military exercises.

According to the source, North Korea's request for technological cooperation goes beyond simply acquiring technology; it appears to be strategically focused on the operational deployment of nuclear weapons.
